speaker: interviewer
text: This tape number seven, interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. Wanted to ask you about a phrase that is commonly used in the context of not just kindertransport but with the Holocaust generally. The term that I'm referring to is survivor guilt. Have you ever felt survivor guilt? Can you comment about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't felt survivor guilt until I knew that I was a survivor. In other words that-- that the others weren't and I was. I would say that a certain amount of guilt is mixed into-- was mixed into my feelings from the time I left my family, I left my father at the station in Berlin in '38. There has to be a certain feeling of-- I am leaving them in the lurch. I am going where it's free, where there's life. I am leaving them in this situation, which is hell And very likely, or very possibly, leads to death. Now, these are not articulated in the mind of a 14-- these words are not articulated in the mind of 14-year-old. But there has to be something of that, there is an unease, which is not only the worry about their fate, but which is also which we've talked about before. But there is also a certain amount of this guilt. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That stays with you. And no doubt stayed with me through the war, less through the war because there was literally nothing I could do. And-- but I knew enough about the situation that-- that-- oh, one has fantasies, everyone has fantasies-- white knight on a white charger riding to the rescue, a Scarlet Pimpernel, that kind of fantasy, of course. But in any real terms, there was absolutely nothing I could do. And so there may be-- ironically enough, the guilt was least in the wartime. And the situation at least was clear-- there was nothing I could do. Afterwards, I did everything I could to find out what had happened. And once likelihood was established, which then grew over the years into a certainty, that they were dead. Then as that became a certainty in my mind-- more of a certainty because it's not even now a total certainty in regard to go to my sister, who was much younger than my parents-- and since there's no absolutely final documentation or any final confirmation of their fate and of her fate particularly. But even then, of course, one has to realize that that's a very forlorn and some hope-- some little bit of hope. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I think the only-- the only kind of guilt is rational guilt. Have I done enough to really find out about them? Because it wouldn't have changed-- would only have changed their subsequent life if they were survivors also, if they had survived it. So I would say that the guilt was-- guilt part of this whole complex of feelings, which of course was very complex, which I didn't know the full extent of until much later-- that this wasn't-- guilt was not a major component of these feelings until it was more certain that they were dead. And when it, of course, comes as the kind of-- now it becomes irrational, even more irrational than before, which says, why should I be alive when they're dead? The kind of grieving that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] reproaches one for being-- having been-- having had the escape that one had. That's a kind of guilt, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never even heard the term survivor guilt until I was seeing a therapist about some problems that I was having with my second wife at the time of our divorce. And we talked about many things. And we talked about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used the term. And I said, what do you mean survivor guilt? I didn't know I was a survivor. Until that time, I thought of survivors-- I thought as survivors, only people that-- that-- I thought only of people that had survived the camps, that have survived in Germany or in enemy territory in hiding or whatever, but that had survived the war under those circumstances as true survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hadn't had any horrors happen to me, personally-- only my family had horrors happen to them. And I have had horrors happen in my mind. But I haven't had horrors happen to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was then-- my eyes were then opened, by not only by this man, who wasn't even Jewish, but by others that I talked about it, I talked about this-- talked with about this subject, what this really meant, and that I had been quite wrong. That there can be guilt and that certain that I hadn't thought of as guilt, is guilt. And that this isn't all that uncommon-- maybe irrational, but it's not uncommon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also that the term survivor covers more people than I thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fact that your early-- your early life was sort of disjointed, it was disrupted, do you believe that that has affected your adult life in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I can say fairly definitely that I am now aware that it has. And I can link up the cause and the effect better than I could before. I've had-- I've been in therapy, my father-- being from this background-- I think nothing of going to the-- to the-- t the therapist if I have--if I have a problem, a psychological problem-- I want to solve it. Same as for pneumonia, I call the doctor. I want to solve that. Same thing. For me, this is the same thing. I don't have resistance to that that some people have. And in the course of the years-- until relatively recently-- I have indeed found-- found out that there is a link. It's very hard to focus on. It's very hard to identify specifically. But generally speaking, it might best be put as a-- an-- that the interruption of familiar life in the middle you childhood robs one of the interchange, interpersonal exchange of conversation, behavior, feelings, all the different aspects of community life in this smallish community called a family. And also it must be said in the larger community around the family, which in normal circumstances somewhat shapes and contributes to the familiar life itself, the circumstances around the family were obviously grossly abnormal and certainly contributed not only in the sense of making people more afraid-- so it contributed very negatively to familial life at the best of times. But even having familiar life at that time interrupted, the exchange between the family members, the things one learns from each other and particularly that the younger ones learn from the other ones-- the thing of manners, how to behave to each other, how to get over fights that one has, negative emotions as well as positive emotions, and so forth-- those are things that are very definitely-- that I missed the-- I have missed in my later what I guess would call-- one would call the development of the affect of the-- of the affect portion of my psyche. I don't know the technical terms so I'm just using the best I can. And specifically, if you will, you can point to my lack of openness, very grave difficulty in expressing emotion, even through to those that one is very close to, emotionally very close to. A result-- not only of that of course, many other things, as well-- has been two broken marriages. That certainly has been contributed by the things that I'm talking about. It's a complex thing. I can only mention a few here. And one of the expression, the feeling certainly, the feeling that one is on one's own, which kind of builds a shell-- and indeed I was on my own. But that started with me at a very young age. And I was on my own. There wasn't anybody I could go to, not really. And so one becomes very self-reliant. And self-reliant in a certain sense doesn't mean anti-societal, but it certainly affects negatively one's relations with society, the communications one has with society, and what one communicates to society about oneself and what one takes in the communication by society to oneself. So again, the same thing applies within the family that one tries to create. And that I've tried twice to create-- unsuccessfully. And part of it is the great negative feelings that one has that I felt. You say grief-- yes, I grieved. But grief was mixed with an enormous hatred, an enormous hatred, just resent and hatred for all that craziness, and the people that expressed that craziness by killing, mass killing, mass extermination, and all the horrors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anger, which I couldn't express. How do I express it? What do I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to control it. I lived in a society at that time, English society, which puts a great premium on the control of feelings-- which maybe was bad for me. Maybe I would have done better in the kind of society that says, hey, let it all hang out. That was not the case in England. And so one could make a virtue of necessity, if you will, and stiff upper lip. Later, I left England and came here, and I was more open to-- to other influences and to-- grew less afraid, if you will, afraid of myself and of my hatred. I was able to open myself up to some of these and acknowledge some of these relationships between cause and effect that I've mentioned, and, of course, many others. But I can say in brief, yes, there is a-- there is a connection. There is a price to pay for one's freedom, and for one's survivorship.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I know what the lesson of the Holocaust is for you but can you express your own feelings about what the Holocaust should mean to the next generation and to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I try not to be too cynical. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that the generally accepted lesson should be-- never again. And my only contribution to that-- to add to that, really, is it's not enough to say it. It's just something that one has to be on a guard and don't go to the other extreme, but try to love everybody. Not love everybody, there's some people we don't like. Doesn't mean we want to kill them, just means we don't like them. We don't choose their company. That's-- that's freedom, too. This business of going and loving everybody, this just doesn't make sense to me in any realistic terms and could lead to a-- to really a kind of a backlash, if you will, away from the very aim that it tries to pursue-- because it's not realistic. It's forcing human nature into a straitjacket, just as much as the other does-- just as much as hatred does. Not as much maybe, no. And it's not as directly damaging, and as directly hurtful, and as directly risky and dangerous. But it is dangerous, also. And so one has to be measured, one has to be realistic, and one has to be very watchful. And one has to learn more about the psychological aspects of this, of the different kinds of government, of the different kinds of society, and draw the right lessons from those. And that's very, very difficult to ask. And I see some progress towards that, certainly. But not as much as I'd like to see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so very, very much for this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much for giving me the opportunity to go on record, as it were. And finally, I'd like to introduce my present wife. And I certainly, fervently hope, my last wife. And her name is Leslie-- Leslie Ford. And we've been married for a while-- as-- as been together for a longer while, as came out in the-- what I said before. And you can also imagine that with everything that has gone before this-- before this moment, that-- and in particularly what I have been saying in the last half hour or so-- that she has a lot to put up with. And that alone-- for that alone, she deserves some kind of award. I won't say order, because that sounds too military and dramatic. But she is exactly what the doctor ordered. And I love her very much. And even if she must find it very difficult sometimes to love me back. But in any case, here she is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've known Henry now for almost 20 years, although we haven't been married that long. And it's been interesting to watch his development over that time. First is recognition of his Jewish heritage, and then his recognition of being a survivor, culminating in his interview today. And that for me has been a very gratifying event.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very early photo of my mother and her younger sister. It was taken at some point during the first decade of the century.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, this is my mother Anna and her sister was Illa. She was called Illa, her name was Alice. This is me in about 1936, so I was just about 12 years old, with my favorite cat. Here my sister and I, we are respectively-- let's see-- 10 and eight years old-- no, eight and six years old, I beg your pardon, with our grandparents, my mother's parents. My grandmother was called Clara. And my grandfather, as I mentioned, Julius. This is my mother as she was just about at the time that I went to England. So she would be 40 years old. And this is my father as he was also at the time I went to England. And he would be about 53 years old at that time. And this is my lovely sister Anneliese, and that must have been also about the same time-- I can't date it exactly-- but she would be around 13 or 14 years old. And this is me, again, during the Berlin time when I would be 12 or 13. The stone portal in the center of the picture is all that's left of the synagogue where I was bar mitzvahed when I was 13. This is in the Fasanenstrasse in Berlin. And around that there has been built a modern building, which now houses the Jewish community in Berlin. And this is me shortly after my arrival, a year or two after my arrival in England, so I was around 15 at that time. This is taken in Bradford, Yorkshire. This is a sample of the-- several samples-- of the Red Cross messages that we were allowed to send to each other during the war, in the early part of the war. And this was to be no more than 25 words, in other words, that was the last paragraph in this message. This is the permit that I mentioned before, which one needed, which a Jew needed, in order to move about Berlin. But only move about in one specific route, which was specified in writing on this permit. If he was found anywhere else on any other transport line, he would be arrested, which is precisely what happened to my sister. This is a document from the Gestapo, confirming the arrest of my parents and showing the circumstances of it and show the various bureaucratic steps that have to be taken following the arrest in regard to the assets and the goods that were in the house and how to dispose of it, et cetera, et cetera. Everything done in very German bureaucratic exactitude. Now we come to happier times. These are my two sons. On the left-- I mean, without glasses, is my son Alexander, who now lives in California. And the boy with glasses is my son Nicholas, my younger son, who is living in Georgia, USA. And now to the future. These are my two grandsons. The older one is Nicky. These are both sons of Alexander. The older one is Nicky, who is now about five. And the little one is Gabriel, who was just born. He was less than a year old at this time.
